title: Making and using clay slab templates
subtitle: Card and clay templates for cutting consistent slab shapes
excerpt: A technique for making reusable card or biscuit-fired clay templates to cut consistent shapes from rolled slabs. Templates eliminate the need to measure and mark each piece individually, speeding up production when making sets or repeated forms.
sourceNotes: Slab templates are a standard pottery workshop tool. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.

content.text: A slab template is a pattern shape used to guide the fettling knife when cutting clay panels. When making a set of four coasters, a tray of matching tiles, or the five panels of a slab box, a template ensures every piece is the same size and shape. It removes the need to measure each piece individually and produces work that fits together cleanly.
Card templates
Card templates are the simplest starting point. Cut the required shape from thick card (cereal box card or watercolour card is ideal). Place the card template on the rolled
slab

and trace around the edge with the fettling knife, keeping the blade vertical and pressed against the card edge. The cut follows the template exactly. Card templates last for several sessions but eventually absorb moisture from the clay and warp; replace when they distort.
Making the template shape
Draw the required shape on card to the actual finished size, accounting for clay shrinkage if the piece will be kiln-fired. For air-dry clay with no firing, cut to the exact finished size.
Cut cleanly with scissors or a craft knife. Straight edges must be straight; curves must be smooth.
Label the template with the project name and the clay thickness it is used with.
Using the template
Roll the clay slab to the required thickness.
Lay the template flat on the slab. The template should not be lifted or slid sideways once placed.
Hold the template firmly with one hand and cut around the edge with the fettling knife held vertically. Draw the knife toward you in one continuous stroke per side.
Lift away the template. Remove the excess clay around the cut shape.
Templates for curved shapes

For curved shapes (ovals, leaf forms, the front face of a wall pocket), trace the card template with the needle tool rather than cutting directly against it. The needle tool scores a visible line into the clay surface; cut along this scored line with the fettling knife in a second pass. This avoids the template shifting when cutting curves.
What it looks like when it is right

Two panels cut from the same template should be identical when laid flat on top of each other. Straight edges align precisely. Any gap between the two pieces when held together indicates the template was moved during cutting.
Common mistakes
Using a warped or bent template, which makes inconsistent cuts.
Angling the knife outward when cutting, producing a bevelled edge rather than a clean perpendicular face.
Not accounting for shrinkage in kiln-fired work. Air-dry clay does not require shrinkage allowance; fired clay shrinks 8-14% depending on the body.
